summary: DeVault argues that free software belongs to everyone, not just its original creators, and that this shared ownership comes with shared responsibility. He pushes back against the entitlement mindset where users expect maintainers to fix bugs and add features for them. Instead, he contends that users are empowered to make changes themselves, and that obstacles in any FOSS project are simply your own problems to solve. The post encourages people to not be intimidated by unfamiliar codebases or languages, and to fork or rewrite projects when necessary. He frames even slow, incremental contributions as meaningful progress.
key_points: Free software belongs to everyone collectively, not to its original authors — Linux doesn't belong to Linus, Firefox doesn't belong to Mozilla
Users are not entitled to bug fixes or features; they are empowered to make those changes themselves
When a bug in an upstream dependency blocks your project, it becomes your problem to fix, not someone else's
Unfamiliar languages, large codebases, and limited time shouldn't be barriers — small contributions add up over long periods
Absentee maintainers and legacy code are solvable problems: fork it or rewrite it
money_quotes: Free software is not theirs - it's ours.
You're not entitled to bug fixes or new features - you're empowered by free software to make those changes yourself.
If a problem in some FOSS project, be it a bug or a conspicuously missing feature, is in the way of your goals, it's your problem.
I guess you're writing a libwayland patch then!
Fork it! Rewrite it! These are tough marbles but they're the marbles you've gotta deal with.
The entire world of free software is your oyster.
FOSS doesn't have to move quickly, it just has to keep moving.
key_insight: Free software's true value is not free-as-in-beer entitlement to support, but the empowerment to take ownership of any problem in any project and fix it yourself.
